text.title: Optimization Tactics by K-Factor Component
text.summary: Concrete tactics for increasing i (invites sent) and c (invitee conversion), ranked by difficulty and expected impact, with share-moment identification guidance.

rule.statement: Place the share prompt at peak-emotion moments (just completed a milestone, just discovered something great) — not in settings menus.
rule.rationale: Share prompts in settings rely on users seeking them out; achievement-moment prompts capture naturally high intent and produce 3–5x higher share rates.

list.item: achievement trigger (completed goal, hit milestone) → celebration modal with share button
discovery trigger (found something valuable) → "share with a friend who'd love this"
social trigger (want to connect) → "invite teammates to collaborate"
help trigger (need someone else to use it) → collaborative feature invite
multi-channel sharing (email, SMS, copy link) — medium / medium
viral content (shareable badges, results images) — medium / high
contact import (bulk invite) — hard / high (privacy constraints; iOS/Android blocked post-2021)
team features that require collaboration — hard / very high

rule.statement: Personal invites ("Anna invited you to [Product]") beat brand invites by 20–40% on click and signup; implement with a single DB join on the referral token.
rule.rationale: Named social proof reduces perceived spam risk and creates accountability, increasing click-through before the landing page is even reached.

list.item: personalize invite landing: show inviter name + avatar + their activity context
add social proof: "join N people using [Product]"
one-click signup: Google/Apple SSO, pre-filled email from invite token
show value before signup: preview of what the invitee will access
mobile-optimize: reduce tap count to ≤3 from invite link to activated account

antipattern.description: Requiring signup to view shared content.
antipattern.reason: Forcing pre-signup registration drops invitee conversion by 40–60%; a preview wall converts better than a hard gate.

rule.statement: Reward at value-realization (invitee reaches activation milestone), not at signup, to cut incentive fraud.
rule.rationale: Signup-triggered rewards are easy to game with fake accounts; activation-triggered rewards require genuine engagement that fraud bots cannot mimic at scale.

list.item: one-time reward: $10 credit after invitee activates
tiered multi-referral: 1 referral → $10, 5 → $50 + badge, 10 → $100 + premium feature
two-sided: both inviter and invitee receive reward — higher acceptance rate
